"' Gaina-sutras, translated by Jacobi, Sacred Books of the East, vol. xxii. Introd. p. xvi.

ON TOLERATION. 39 After a time, however, Buddhism seems to have spread very rapidly, and to have led to great social and political changes. The dynasty founded by /fandragupta, at the time of Alexander the Great, was that of a pretender, a kind of Napoleon, not belonging by birth to the royal caste, and therefore bidding for popular support, wherever he could find it. It is possible that the Buddhists were more ready to recognise and support him than the orthodox Brahman s. Anyhow, under the reign of his grandson, king Asoka (259-222 b. c), we see the followers of Buddha not only recognised, but patronised by the king, and we hear of a great Council held under his auspices to settle the sacred canon of the Buddhists.
Toleration preached by Buddhism.

In some respects Buddhism may be called a kind of Protestantism directed against Brahmanism, and we know that neither those who protest nor those who are protested against, are generally distinguished by mutual love and charity. The first authentic evidence of the political and social changes produced by the spreading of Buddhism in India, we have in those wonderful inscriptions of king Asoka, which are scattered over the whole of his dominions. They have suffered, no doubt, during the more than two thousand years that they have been exposed to the climate of India ; but, as they exist in many copies, their text has been restored, they have been published and interpreted, and we can now read them exactly as they were read by the subjects of king Asoka, whether Buddhists or Brahmans. Their decipherment is due to the combined labours of Prinsep, 40 LECTURE ir.

Burnouf, Wilson, Norris, Biihler, and Senart. If then we remember that A.soka was a kind of Constantine, who owed much of his power to the support which he received from the new religion, or, if you like^ a kind of Henry VIII, hated by all who suffered under the new reform, you will be surprised to see how much more of true Christian charity was shown by this Buddhist king in the third century b. c. than by Constantine in the fourth, or by Henry VIII in the sixteenth century.
The Edicts of Asoka.

It may be true that the idea of putting up public inscriptions struck the Indian mind for the first time when Greek ideas had reached India after Alexander's conquests. But when Asoka had his inscriptions engraved on rocks and pillars, it was not in order to perpetuate his name or the names of his ancestors, it was not to glorify his own origin ; it was chiefly to preach toleration for all creeds. Thus he says in his Seventh Edict ^ :

' The king Piyadasi, dear to the gods, desires that all sects should dwell (in peace) everywhere ; for they all desire the control of the senses and purity of mind. Men, however, have different wishes and different passions ; they will perform the whole or a part only (of what they ought to do). But even he whose charity is not abundant, may surely always possess control of the senses, purity of mind, gratitude, and loyalty I'
' Senart, Les inscriptions de Piyadasi, Pai'is, 1881, vol. i. p. 174. Buhler, Zeitschrift der D. M. G., xxxvii. p. 279.

'^ Buhler takes nifee, not for nityam, but for nlka, low, and ON TOLERATION. 41 And again, in his Twelfth Edict (1. c, p. 251), he says: 'The king Piyadasi, dear to the gods, honours all sects, those of hermits and those of householders, he honours them by alms and by different kinds of worship. But the king, dear to the gods, does not value alms and worship as much as the increase of essentials. And^ the increase of essentials does not differ much in different sects ; on the contrary, the true foundation of every one consists in the bridling of the tongue, so that neither should there be praising of one's own sect, nor disparagement of other sects, without a cause ; and whenever there is cause, it should be moderate ^. The religions of others may even be praised for any given reason^. In this manner a man may much advantage his own sect, and at the same time benefit that of others ; while if he acts otherwise, he damages his own sect and injures that of others. For whosoever exalts his own sect and disparages all others from a strong devotion to his own sect, but, in truth, from a wish that it should be rendered pre-eminent, he will, in this wise, injure his own sect much more. Therefore a mutual understanding is best, that all should listen to the teaching of others, and wish to listen. This is indeed the wish of the king, dear to the gods, that all sects should listen much, and be possessed of pure doctrines. And all who belong to this or to that translates : ' Even in a low person, to whom great liberality is not (possible), control of the senses, purification of the heart, gratitude, and loyalty are (something) beautiful.'
^ Biihler translates : ' The increase of the kernel of all sects (happiness) in different ways ; but its root is prudence in speech.'
^ Here I follow Biihler's translation.
^ According to Biihler's conjecture, tena tena pakalanena.
42 LECTURE ir.
sect, should have it said to them that the king, dear to the gods, does not value alms and worship as much as the increase of essentials, and that respect should be shown to all sects ^.'
The Tolerant Spirit of A.«oka.

The exact meaning of these edicts, which king Asoka had engraved on rocks and pillars in different parts of his kingdom, is not always easy to discover, nor easy to express in our modern terminology. Still, we cannot be mistaken in giving him credit for a most tolerant spirit which finds but few parallels in ancient or modern history. He had recognised what many find so difficult to recognise even now, that we must distinguish between what is essential in all religions, what Asoka calls the sara or sap. and what is not. The number of religious and philosophical sects prevalent in India during the third century before our era must have been very considerable, and on some points their differences were no doubt very great. But Asoka gives them all credit for inculcating the same lesson, namely, control of the passions, purity of mind, gratitude, and loyalty. And think what would be gained, if these four points were really gained by any religion ! And when he comes to what he calls the root or life-spring of religion, he finds it in mutual forbearance, and more particularly in the bridling of the tongue, as if anticipating the well- ^ Biihler translates the last line by : ' as that there should be an increase of the kernel with all sects, and a considerable one,* taking bahuka as an adjective referring to sfiravadhi.
Professor Biihler has lately given a new translation of this twelfth edict from the Shahbhazgari version, which differs in some passages from the version given in the text.
ON TOLEEATION. 43 known words : ' If any man among you seem to be religious, and bridleth not bis tongue, but deceiveth bis own beart, tbis man's religion is vain ^.'

I doubt wbetber any otber religion could produce sucb royal edicts in favour of mutual toleration. Tbey are one of tbe brigbtest pages in tbe bistory of India, and I tbougbt it rigbt to make you acquainted witb tbem, because tbey sbow tbat a comparative study of religions bas some important lessons to teacb, even to us. If tberefore I may sometimes seem to speak too favourably of some of tbese religions^ may I not appeal to tbe words of king Asoka : ' For wbosoever exalts bis own faitb and disparages all otbers from a strong devotion to bis own, be will injure bis own faitb.' And again : ' Tbe true foundation of every faitb consists in bridling the tongue, so tbat tbere sbould be neitber a praising of one's own religion, nor disparagement of otbers.'
Disappearance of Buddhism in India.

Wbetber tbese edicts of Asoka were always obeyed, is anotber question ; but even as an aspiration, tbey deserve our respect. History, so far as we can speak of bistory in India, certainly seems to teacb tbat tbe sentiments of forbearance and brotberly love, inculcated in tbese edicts, bave not always been obeyed by tbe rulers and by tbe people of tbat country. Tbe ruins of Buddbist monasteries, tombs, monuments, and pillars, scattered over tbe wbole of India, tell a terrible tale. For some centuries tbe Buddbist religion must bave ruled supreme in India. During tbe period covered by tbe travels of tbe Cbinese pilgrims, 1 Ep. St. James, i. 26.

from 400 to 700, though the kings still preached toleration, and encouraged peaceful congresses for the discussion of religious questions, we hear already of many Viharas or monasteries being deserted, of many Stiipas, or pillars, lying prostrate. How Buddhism was exterminated in India, we shall never know. But the fact remains that Buddhism exists no longer as an Indian religion. It lives in Ceylon, from whence it has spread in its southern branch to Burma and Siam, and it lives in Nepal, from whence in its northern branch it spread to Tibet, Mongolia, China, and Japan. But in India proper Buddhism has ceased to exist, and the religious census returns no Buddhists except as strangers and pilgrims in their Holy Land, the birth-place of Buddha.

So much for kino- Asoka, the great ruler of India in the third century B. c. We have to remember so many kings for their intolerance. Let us give to king Asoka a niche in our memory for his tolerant spirit, for his benevolence, his large-heartedness, — for that broad and wide view of religion which in our days is so often stigmatised as latitudinarianism.

It seems often to be supposed that if we praise one religion, we tacitly blame others. That is not so. If I call Buddhism tolerant, I do not mean to imply that Christianity is intolerant. Some who call themselves Christians may be intolerant, but the spirit of Christianity itself is not so. Those who so often quote the words (Matth. xii. 30), ' He that is not with me, is against me,' forget the words (Mark ix. 40), 'For he that is not against us, is on our part.'
ON TOLEKATION. 45 Toleration in the Jewish Religfion.

The greatest inincls, whatever their religion, have always been the most tolerant and charitable. We often see how the founders of new religions endeavoured to retain all that was good and true in the religions from which they seceded. They came to fulfil, not to destroy.

Even the Jewish religion, which is often represented as ver}^ intolerant, was not so from the first. The Mosaic Law commanded that the stranger should not be oppressed — ' for you know the heart of a stranger, seeing you were strangers in the land of Egypt.' The Jews were told : ' Ye shall have one law as well for the stranger as for one of your own country.' Now in the eyes of a Jew a stranger was a man who worshipped false gods, and yet Moses claimed toleration and protection for him.

It was owing to political circumstances that the Jewish religion became in time so strongly national and exclusive, and it was the influence of the Rabbis that imparted to it in later times so narrow-minded and dogmatic a character.
zh: ^ 歐登堡，《佛陀》，第 69 頁。
《耆那教經典》（Gaina-sutras），雅可比譯，《東方聖典》（Sacred Books of the East）第二十二冊，導論第十六頁。

39 然而，經過一段時間後，佛教似乎傳播極為迅速，並導致了重大的社會與政治變化。旃陀羅笈多（Chandragupta）建立的王朝——適逢亞歷山大大帝時代——乃是一位篡位者，一種拿破崙式的人物，非出身於王族種姓，因此無論在何處尋求民眾支持。佛教徒很可能比正統的婆羅門更樂於承認並支持他。無論如何，在其孫王阿育王（Asoka，西元前 259-222 年）的統治下，我們不僅看到佛陀的追隨者獲得認可，更受到國王的庇護，並聽聞有一次偉大的會議在其主持下舉行，以便確定佛教的聖典。
佛教所宣揚的寬容

在某些方面，佛教可被稱為針對婆羅門教的一種新教運動，而我們知道，無論是提出抗議的人，還是遭受抗議的人，通常都不會因為相互的愛與慈悲而著稱。佛教在印度傳播所引起的政治與社會變化的第一份真實證據，乃是散佈於阿育王整個統治領域內那些奇蹟般的銘文。這些銘文無疑在暴露於印度氣候逾兩千年的時間中遭受了損害；但由於它們存在許多副本，其文本已得以復原，已經出版並被詮釋，我們現在可以完全按照阿育王的臣民（無論是佛教徒還是婆羅門）當時所閱讀的方式來誦讀它們。它們的譯解乃是由普林塞普的聯合勞績而來。
布爾努夫、威爾遜、諾里斯、比勒和塞納爾。如果我們記得阿育王（A.soka）是一種君士坦丁，他的權力在很大程度上得益於他從這個新宗教所獲得的支持，或者，如果你願意的話，是一種亨利八世，為所有在新改革下蒙受苦難的人所憎恨，那麼你會驚訝於這位佛教國王在西元前第三世紀所展現的真正基督教慈悲精神，遠比君士坦丁在第四世紀或亨利八世在第十六世紀所展現的還要豐富。
阿育王的敕令
誠然，豎立公開銘文的想法可能是在亞歷山大征服後希臘思想傳入印度時，才首次進入印度人的心中。但當阿育王讓其銘文刻於岩石和柱子上時，其目的並非為了永恆保存他的名字或其祖先的名字，也不是為了美化他自身的出身；其主要目的乃是倡導對所有信仰的寬容。因此他在《第七敕令》中說道^：
國王毗耶阿育（Piyadasi）蒙神所寵，願一切教派無處不安居（於和平之中）；蓋彼等皆欲控馭感官且潔淨心靈。然而人心各異，慾望亦殊；彼等或行其全部所應為者，或僅行其一部。然縱其施捨不豐，亦必能時時保有控馭感官、潔淨心靈、感恩與忠貞之心。
塞納爾，《皮耶達西銘文集》，巴黎，1881年，第一卷，第174頁。布勒，《德國東方學會雜誌》，第三十七卷，第279頁。

布勒（Buhler）將 nifee 解釋為非 nityam，而是 nīka（低微），涉及**寬容問題**。41 又在其《第十二詔令》（同上，頁 251）中云：「國王毘耶笈多（皤陀婆羅笈多），蒙諸天眷愛者，恭敬一切教派，無論隱士教派或在家教派，皆以布施與各種崇拜方式敬之。然蒙諸天眷愛之國王，未如重視根本要素之增進。而根本要素之增進，於各教派間並無大異；反之，每一教派之真正基礎，在於制約其舌，俾既不讚揚自宗，亦不無故貶損他宗；縱有所因，亦當有節制之^。他人之宗教，為任何既定之理由，甚至可以稱頌之^。如是行者，一方可大益於自宗，同時亦可利益他宗；若行其反，則損己宗而害他宗。蓋凡人高舉自宗而貶損諸他宗，雖以篤信自宗為名，實則希冀自宗超越他宗，如此行者，益損自宗甚巨。是故相互理解最善，咸應諦聽他宗教法，且願聞之。此實蒙諸天眷愛之國王之願，願諸教派多聞而具清淨教義。凡屬此教派或彼教派者，譯為：『即於施德有限之卑微者，亦能成就感官之制御、心念之淨化、感恩與忠誠，乃為美德。』
^ 比勒爾譯作：「各派對一切幸福之核心的增長方式各有不同，但其根源是言語中的慎思。」
^ 此處我採用比勒爾（Biihler）之譯本。
^ 據毕勒的推測，tena tena pakalanena。
第二講
教派的人應該聽聞，為眾神所鍾愛的國王並不像看重物資增長那樣看重施捨和崇拜，而且應該對所有教派表示尊重^。

A.«oka, the greatest of the ancient sovereigns of India, ruled over an empire which, at the height of its power, extended from the mouth of the Indus to the confines of Assam, and from the Himalaya to the Deccan. He reigned in the third century before the Christian era, and during a reign of about thirty-seven years he left behind him monuments of his wisdom and benevolence which are still extant, and which throw more light on the condition of ancient India than any other documents which we possess. A.«oka began his reign as a zealous worshipper of the Brahmanical religion; but after the conquest of Kalinga, when he witnessed the horrors of war, he underwent a great change of heart. He embraced Buddhism, and became one of the most devoted promoters of that religion. Yet, notwithstanding his zealous adoption of Buddhism, he never became a persecutor of the followers of the Brahmanical faith. On the contrary, he protected them, and granted them privileges and immunities. His edicts, which have been preserved to us, show that he regarded all forms of religion with equal respect, and that he endeavoured to promote virtue and morality, irrespective of the particular form of religious belief. His tolerant spirit was not the result of indifference to religion, but of a deep conviction that all religions contain some truth, and that the essence of all religion consists in the practice of virtue and the cultivation of morality. In one of his edicts he expressed his conviction in the following words: "Let men of all religions live together in my dominions in concord and harmony. Whoever honours his own religion and disparages that of others does it indeed from devotion to his own; but he thereby injures his own religion." 阿育王是古代印度最偉大的統治者，其統治的帝國在權力高峰時期，東起印度河口，西至阿薩姆邊界，北自喜馬拉雅山，南達德干高原。他統治於基督紀元前第三世紀，在約三十七年的統治期間，留下了許多智慧與仁慈的紀念碑，至今仍然存世，並比我們所擁有的任何其他文獻都更能照亮古代印度的面貌。 阿育王統治初期是婆羅門教的虔誠信徒；但在征服羯陵迦後，當他親眼目睹戰爭的恐怖時，他的心靈經歷了巨大的轉變。他皈依了佛教，並成為該宗教最虔誠的推崇者之一。然而，儘管他熱切地採納了佛教，他卻從未成為婆羅門教信徒的迫害者。相反地，他保護他們，並授予他們特權與豁免權。他流傳下來的敕令顯示，他以同等的尊重看待所有的宗教形式，並且他致力於推進道德與倫理，不因特定的宗教信仰形式而異。 他的寬容精神並非源於對宗教的漠不關心，而是源於深刻的信念，即所有宗教都包含某些真理，而一切宗教的本質都在於實踐道德與修養倫理。在他的一份敕令中，他用以下言辭表達了他的信念：「願所有宗教的信徒在我的統治範圍內和睦共處。凡尊崇自己的宗教卻貶低他人的宗教者，雖然出於對自己宗教的虔誠，但實際上卻傷害了自己的宗教。」

阿育王在其帝國各地的岩石與柱子上刻製的這些敕令，其確切含義往往不易發現，也不易用現代術語表達。然而，我們無法否認應當稱讚他所展現的最為寬容的精神——這種精神在古代或現代歷史上都鮮有其匹。他已認識到許多人至今仍難以認識的事實，即我們必須區分所有宗教中的本質要素——阿育王所謂的娑羅（sara）或精髓——與非本質要素。第三世紀前我們這個時代的印度流行的宗教與哲學派系數量必然相當龐大，而在某些要點上它們的分歧想必甚為巨大。然而阿育王將信譽歸功於它們全部，因為它們都在傳授同樣的教訓，即對情慾的節制、心靈的純淨、感恩之心與忠誠。試想，如果任何宗教真正獲得了這四點，將會獲致什麼樣的成果！當他談及他所謂的宗教的根源或生命泉源時，他將之發現於相互寬容之中，尤其是發現於對舌頭的制約之中，彷彿預見了——^ 比勒爾（Bühler）將最後一行翻譯為：「使得所有派系的核心有所增長，且增長相當可觀」，將 bahuka 理解為修飾 śravadhí 的形容詞。
畢勒教授最近根據《沙巴茲加里》抄本版本對這道詔勅進行了新的翻譯，其在某些段落上異於正文所引述的版本。
論寬容。43 已知的詞語：「你們中間若有人自以為虔誠，卻不勒住舌頭，反而欺騙自己的心，這人的虔誠就是虛妄的¹。」

我懷疑是否有任何其他宗教能夠頒布如此維護相互寬容的皇家敕令。這些敕令是印度歷史上最光輝燦爛的篇章之一，我認為有必要讓諸位了解它們，因為它們表明，即使對我們而言，比較宗教學的研究也有重要的教訓可以傳授。因此，如果我有時似乎對某些宗教評價過高，我是否可以引用阿育王（King Asoka）的言辭：「凡是出於對自己信仰的強烈虔誠而推崇自己的信仰、貶低所有他人的，將傷害自己的信仰。」又言：「每一種信仰的真正基礎在於控制舌頭，如此既不應讚頌自己的宗教，亦不應貶低他人的宗教。」
佛教在印度的消亡

阿育王的這些敕令是否始終被人遵守，這是另一個問題；但即使作為一種願景，它們也值得我們尊敬。就我們能夠談論印度歷史而言，歷史確實似乎教導我們，這些敕令中所闡述的寬容與兄弟友愛的情感，並未始終為該國的統治者與人民所遵行。散佈於整個印度的佛教寺院、陵墓、紀念碑與石柱的遺跡，述說著一個令人悲哀的故事。在某些世紀中，佛教必定曾在印度佔據至高無上的統治地位。在中國朝聖者旅行所涵蓋的時期，1 聖雅各書，第一章第二十六節。

從西元四百至七百年間，雖然諸王仍然宣揚寬容，並鼓勵和平的宗教討論大會，但我們已聽聞許多毘訶羅（Vihara）或僧院遭到遺棄，許多窣堵波（Stûpa）或塔柱傾圮倒地。佛教如何在印度被根除，我們將永遠無法知曉。但事實仍然存在：佛教作為一種印度宗教已經不復存在。它存活於錫蘭，從錫蘭向南傳入其南傳分支流傳到緬甸和暹羅，它也存活於尼泊爾，從尼泊爾向北傳入其北傳分支流傳到西藏、蒙古、中國和日本。但在印度本土，佛教已經消亡，宗教人口統計中除了作為佛陀聖地——佛陀誕生地——中的陌生人和朝聖者外，已無佛教徒的記錄。
關於動態電影阿育王就說到這裡——他是西元前三世紀印度的偉大統治者。我們必須記住許多國王因其不寬容而聲名狼籍。讓我們在記憶中為阿育王開闢一席之地，銘記他寬容的精神、他的仁慈、他的博大胸懷——銘記那種宗教觀念的廣博與寬闊，在我們這個時代常常被貶低為寬鬆主義（latitudinarianism）。
人們往往以為，如果我們讚美某一宗教，就會暗中貶低其他宗教。事實並非如此。若我稱佛教為寬容的，並不意味著我要暗示基督教是不寬容的。某些自稱基督徒的人也許不寬容，但基督教本身的精神卻並非如此。那些常常引述《馬太福音》第十二章第三十節「不與我同心的，就是敵我的」的人，往往遺忘了《馬可福音》第九章第四十節的言辭：「因為不敵擋我們的，就是幫助我們的。」
論寬容 45 猶太教中的寬容
無論信奉何種宗教，最偉大的人物始終是最寬容、最慈悲的。我們經常看到新宗教的創立者們如何努力保留他們所脫離之宗教中一切美善與真理的內容。他們來此是為了完成，而非摧毀。
即使是經常被描繪為十分不寬容的猶太宗教，從一開始也並非如此。摩西律法命令人們不得欺壓陌生人——「你們知道寄居者的心境，因為你們在埃及地也曾為寄居者。」猶太人被告知：「無論是寄居者，或是本地人，你們都要有一樣的律法。」在猶太人的眼中，陌生人就是那些崇拜假神的人，然而摩西卻為他爭取了寬容與保護。
猶太宗教之所以最終變得如此強烈的民族性與排他性，乃是由於政治環境的影響；而後來它所呈現出的狹隘與教條化特徵，則是拉比們的影響所致。
